Every Angular codebase I have ever audited cancels HTTP requests in one of three ways.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Two of them are bugs wearing a disguise.
I know this because I spent a week hunting a memory leak in a dashboard that polled six endpoints. The codebase used all three cancellation styles, written by three different developers across three years. One style leaked. One style worked but buried the team in boilerplate. One style made the entire problem disappear.
The uncomfortable part? The leaking style is the one most tutorials teach first. The style that solved everything is the one most tutorials never reach.
Here are all three, ranked from worst to best, with the receipts from that dashboard. By the end you will know exactly which one your codebase uses, and if it is the wrong one, you will know what Monday morning looks like.
First, What “Cancel” Actually Means in Angular
One piece of machinery before the ranking, because all three ways depend on it.
An HttpClient observable is cold. Nothing happens until you subscribe, and, the part most developers never learn, the subscription owns the request. End the subscription and Angular runs a teardown that calls abort on the…
